senses.g: indigo (plant from which the bluish dye is obtained)

Nile (a large river in Africa, usually considered to be the longest river in the world; its basin covers Burundi, Rwanda, Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia, Eritrea, South Sudan, Sudan, and Egypt, flowing north through Khartoum and Cairo into the Mediterranean Sea)

ex.u: دریائے نیل
ex.r: daryā-e Nīl
ex.e: the Nile river

نیل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian نیل (nīl), from Arabic النِيل (an-nīl).

senses.g: nail polish, a cosmetic lacquer applied to the fingernails or toenails
auction (a public event where goods are sold through bidding)

نیلام.e: Borrowed from Portuguese leilão, borrowed from Arabic إِعْلَام (ʔiʕlām), from أَعْلَمَ (ʔaʕlama). Doublet of اِعْلام (i'lām).

senses.g: any self-imposed restraint or religious observance

نین.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit नयन (nayana), from the root नी (nī), from Proto-Indo-European *neyH-.

ex.u: ہم سو نہیں رہے ہیں۔ ہم سو کیسے سکتے ہیں ،اُس بوڑھے نے تو ہماری نیندیں اُڑا دی ہیں۔
ex.r: ham so nahī̃ rahe ha͠i. ham so kaise sakte ha͠i, us būṛhe ne to hamārī nīndẽ uṛā dī ha͠i.
ex.e: We're not sleeping. How can we sleep? That old man has crashed our sleep.

نیند.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi نیند (nīnd) / نید (nīd), from Old Hindi نیند (nīnd /⁠nynd⁠/) / نید (nīd /⁠nyd⁠/) / نندا (nindā), from Prakrit 𑀡𑀺𑀤𑁆𑀤𑀸 (ṇiddā), from Sanskrit निद्रा (nidrā́), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *nidráH.

senses.g: New York, New York City (the largest city in the state of New York, and the largest city in the United States, a metropolis extending into neighboring New Jersey in addition to Pennsylvania and Connecticut)
New York (a state of the United States)
New Zealand (a country in Oceania; capital: Wellington)
inflection of نیا (nayā, “new”): — feminine singular direct
inflection of نیا (nayā, “new”): — feminine singular oblique/vocative
inflection of نیا (nayā, “new”): — feminine plural direct
inflection of نیا (nayā, “new”): — feminine plural oblique/vocative
New Delhi (the capital city of India, located in the national capital territory of Delhi)

نئی دہلی.e: Calque of Hindi नई दिल्ली (naī dillī), equivalent to نئی (naī, “new”) + دہلی (dihlī, “Delhi”). Compare Punjabi نَوِیْں دِلّی (nauiīṉ dillī) / Punjabi ਨਵੀਂ ਦਿੱਲੀ (navī̃ dillī).

senses.g: The marker of the ergative case with perfective-aspect transitive verbs

ex.u: لڑکے نے کتاب خریدی۔
ex.r: laṛke ne kitāb xarīdī.
ex.e: The boy bought a book.

senses.g: A letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ن and followed by و (w, o, ū), representing nasalization of the previous vowel at the end of a word.
The thirty-third letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ں and followed by ہ.

و.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian و (wa, u).

واپس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian واپس (wāpas). Compare Punjabi ਵਾਪਸ (vāpas).

واپسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian واپسی (wāpasī), from واپس (wāpas, “returned”), whence واپس (vāpas).

واجب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian واجب, from Arabic وَاجِب (wājib).

senses.g: condemned to death; liable to death

واحد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَاحِد (wāhid), from Arabic وَاحِد (wāḥid).

senses.g: a title for a landlord, especially in Sindh, Pakistan

وادی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وادی (wādī), from Arabic وَادٍ (wādin).

وادیٔ سندھ کی تہذیب.e: From Urdu وادی (vādī, “valley”) + Urdu سندھ (“Sindh”) + Urdu کی (kī, “of”) + Urdu تہذیب (“civilization”).

وار.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀸𑀟𑁄 (pāḍo), from Sanskrit पात (pāta, “attack”).

وارانسی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit वाराणसी (vārāṇasī). Doublet of بنارس (banāras).

وارث.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَارِث (wāris), from Arabic وَارِث (wāriṯ).

واسی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit वासिन् (vāsin). Related to بسنا (basnā, “to live, reside”).
Borrowed from Sanskrit वासिन् (vāsin). Related to بسنا (basnā, “to live, reside”).

senses.g: Washuk (a city in Washuk district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

واقع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَاقِع (wāqi'), from Arabic وَاقِع (wāqiʕ).

واقعہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian واقعه (vâqi'a), itself from Arabic وَاقِعَة (wāqiʕa).

واقعی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian واقعی (wāqi'ī), from Arabic وَاقِعِيّ (wāqiʕiyy).

senses.g: really, truly, verily, certainly, actually, indeed

واقعی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian واقعی (wāqi'ī), from Arabic وَاقِعِيّ (wāqiʕiyy).

senses.g: aware of, knowing, conversant, informed, sensible, experienced, learned

والد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَالِد (wālid), from Arabic وَالِد (wālid).

والدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian والده (wālida), from Arabic وَالِدَة (wālida).

والدین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَالِدَیْن (wālidayn), itself from Arabic وَالِدَيْن (wālidayn).

وامن.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit वामन (vāmaná). Doublet of بَونَا (baunā).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit वामन (vāmaná). Doublet of بَونَا (baunā).

senses.g: name of the elephant that supports the south or west quarter

وامن.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit वामन (vāmaná). Doublet of بَونَا (baunā).

senses.g: forms adjectives and nouns which are properly masculines, chiefly from nouns, having the sense possessed of

ex.u: بھاگْیَہ (bhāgya, “fortune”) + وان (vān) → بھاگْیَہ وان (bhāgya vān, “fortunate”)

وان.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit -वान् (-vān), masculine nominative singular of -वत् (-vat).

senses.g: -th (suffix used to form ordinals from numerals)
Wah (a city in Rawalpindi district, Punjab, Pakistan)

وایو.e: From Sanskrit वायु (vāyu), Proto-Indo-Iranian, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂weh₁yu- (“wind, air”), ultimately from *h₂weh₁- (“to blow”).

وبا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وبا (wabā), from Arabic وَباء (wabāʔ).

senses.g: a weight on the shoulders; a burden of guilt

ex.u:
شوریدگی کے ہاتھ سے ہے سر وبال دوش
صحرا میں اے خدا کوئی دیوار بھی نہیں
śorīdgī ke hāth se hai sar vabāl-e-doś
ex.r: sahrā mẽ ai xudā koī dīvār bhī nahī̃
ex.e:
in the hands of madness/passion, the head is a weight on the shoulders/burden of guilt
in (this) desert, oh Lord, there are not even any walls!

وبال دوش.e: Izafa compound of وبال (vabāl, “burden, weight”) + دوش (doś). The second term in the compound conflates borrowings from both Classical Persian دوش (dōš, “shoulder”) and Sanskrit दोष (doṣa, “guilt, fault, blame”).

ex.u: ڈھاکہ یونیورسٹی کی اردو وِبھاگ
ex.e: Urdu department of Dhaka University

senses.g: the divisions of a tala, a vibhag

وبھاگ.e: It is made up with prefix ب and term بھاگ

senses.g: Witr, a salah done after Isha and before Fajr

وجود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وُجُود (wujūd), borrowed from Arabic وُجُود (wujūd).

senses.g: mode, manner, reason, ratio; way, method, plan; cause, means

ex.u: تم نے یہ کس وجہ سے کیا؟
ex.r: tum ne ye kis vaja se kiyā?
ex.e: For what reason did you do this?

senses.g: means of subsistence; wages, salary; money

وجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَجْه (wajh), from Arabic وَجْه (wajh).

senses.g: word, speech, statement, oath, promise, pledge
a female given name, Waheeda, from Arabic, masculine equivalent وَحِید (vahīd)

وحیدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَحِیدَه (wahīda), from Arabic وَحِيدَة (waḥīda).

ودکا.e: Borrowed from English vodka, ultimately from Russian во́дка (vódka).

senses.g: Vidhan Sabha: a state legislative assembly in India
Wadh (a city in Khuzdar district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

وراثت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وِرَاثَت (wirāsat), from Arabic وِرَاثَة (wirāṯa).

ورزش بازی.e: From Classical Persian ورزش بازی (warziš bāzī).

وزارت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وِزارَت (wizārat), from Arabic وِزَارَة (wizāra).

وزن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وزن (wazn), from Arabic وَزْن (wazn).

ex.u: وَزِیرِ تِجارَت
ex.r: vazīr-i tijārat
ex.e: minister of commerce

وزیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَزیر (vazīr), from Arabic وَزِير (wazīr).

وزیر اعظم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَزِیرِ اَعْظَم (wazīr-i a'zam). By surface analysis, وَزِیر (vazīr, “minister”) + ِ (i, “ezafe construction”) + اَعْظَم (a'zam, “grand, great”).

وزیر اعلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَزِیرِ اَعْلیٰ (wazīr-i a'lā). By surface analysis, وَزِیر (vazīr, “minister”) + ِ (i, “ezafe construction”) + اَعْلیٰ (a'lā, “supreme”).

senses.g: Wazirabad (a city in Wazirabad district, Punjab, Pakistan)

وزیر خارجہ.e: Calque of Arabic وَزِير الْخَارِجِيَّة (wazīr al-ḵārijiyya).

senses.g: Waziristan (a mountainous region in northwestern Pakistan)
consigning, handing over — the immersion of an idol into water

ex.u: گنیش وسرجن
ex.r: gaṇeś visarjan
ex.e: the practice of immersing an idol of Ganesh in the water on Anant Chaturdashi

وسعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وُسعَت, from Arabic وُسْعَة (wusʕa).

senses.g: Another title for సామంత in certain dialects of Dekhanim.
woad, indigo (dye), historically used as an eyebrow dye in Iran, Azerbaijan and South Asia

وسمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وسمه (wasma), from Arabic وَسْمَة (wasma).

senses.g: an evening or springtime raga of the Purvi thaat

وسنت.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit वसन्त (vasantá). Doublet of بَہار (bahār) and بَسَنْت (basant).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit वसन्त (vasantá). Doublet of بَہار (bahār) and بَسَنْت (basant).

وسیع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَسِیع (wasī'), from Arabic وَسِيع.

senses.g: a male given name, Vishnu, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

وشواس.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit विश्वास (viśvāsá). First attested as Old Hindi विश्वास (viśvāsa). Doublet of بِسْواس (bisvās).

ex.u:
وہ فراق اور وہ وصال کہاں
وہ شب و روز و ماہ و سال کہاں
voh firāq aur voh visāl kahā̃
ex.r: voh śab-o-roz o māh-o-sāl kahā̃
ex.e:
that separation and that reunion, where?
those nights and days and months and years, where?

وصیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَصِیَّت (wasiyyat), from Arabic وَصِيَّة (waṣiyya).

وضع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَضْع (waz'), from Arabic وَضْع (waḍʕ). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi وضع (vẓ').

senses.g: wudu (a ritual washing of the forearms, head, and feet, required after minor impurity, frequently performed immediately before prayer)

وضو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وُضُو (wuzū), borrowed from Arabic وُضُوء (wuḍūʔ).

ex.u:
مذہب نہیں سکھاتا آپس میں بیر رکھنا
ہندی ہیں ہم وطن ہے ہندوستاں ہمارا
mazhab nahī̃ sikhātā, āpas mẽ bair rakhnā
hindī ha͠i ham, vatan ha͠i, hindostā̃ hamārā
Religion does not preach animosity among ourselves.
ex.r: We are Hindi and our homeland is Hindustan.

وعدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَعْدَه (wa'da), borrowed from Arabic وَعْدَة (waʕda).

senses.g: inflection of وَعْدَہ (va'da, “promise; vow; pledge”): — direct plural
inflection of وَعْدَہ (va'da, “promise; vow; pledge”): — oblique/vocative singular

وغیرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian و غیره (wa ġayra), from Arabic وَغَيْرُهُ (waḡayruhu, literally “and the other(s)”).

وفا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَفَا (wafā), borrowed from Arabic وَفَاء (wafāʔ), from وَفَى (wafā). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi وفا (vfa /⁠vafā⁠/).

senses.g: a female given name from Arabic

وفا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَفَا (wafā), borrowed from Arabic وَفَاء (wafāʔ), from وَفَى (wafā). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi وفا (vfa /⁠vafā⁠/).

وفات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَفَات (wafāt), from Arabic وَفَاة (wafāh).

وفد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَفْد (vafd), from Arabic وَفْد (wafd).

وقار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَقَار (waqār), borrowed from Arabic وَقَار (waqār), from Proto-Semitic *waqar-. First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi وقار (vqar /⁠vaqār⁠/).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

وقار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَقَار (waqār), borrowed from Arabic وَقَار (waqār), from Proto-Semitic *waqar-. First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi وقار (vqar /⁠vaqār⁠/).

وقف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وقف (waqf), from Arabic وَقْف (waqf).
Borrowed from Classical Persian وقف (waqf), from Arabic وَقْف (waqf).

وقفہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَقْفَه (waqfa), from Arabic وَقْفَة (waqfa).

senses.g: province, state, realm, inhabited country or region
a foreign country, especially one in the West (Europe and North America)
England (a constituent country of the United Kingdom)
Great Britain (a large island of the United Kingdom in Northern Europe)
United Kingdom (a kingdom and country in Northern Europe)

ولایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وِلَایَت (wilāyat), from Arabic وِلَايَة (wilāya). Compare Marathi विलायत (vilāyat), Bengali বিলাত (bilat) / বিলেত (bilet).

ولایتی بینگن.e: From وِلایَتی (vilāyatī, “foreign”) and بَین٘گَن (baiṅgan, “eggplant, aubergine”). Cognate with Bengali বিলাতী বেগুন (bilatī begun). Compare with Persian گُوْجِهفَرَنْگی (gowje-farangi, “European plum”) and Dari بادِنْجانِ رومِی (bādinjān-e rōmī, “Roman aubergine”).

senses.g: Holland, the Netherlands (a country in Western Europe)
name of a monkeydemon slain by Balarama.

ولی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ولی (walī), from Arabic وَلِيّ (waliyy).

senses.g: Vile Parle (a suburb of Mumbai, Maharashtra, India)

ex.u: وِلے پارْلے کے ہِی نام پَر کَمْپَنِی کا نام بِھی پارْلے رَکھا گَیا
ex.r: koronā vāiras: pārle jī kī rekārḍ faroxt, lāk ḍāun mẽ biskiṭ kampaniyõ kā bol bālā
ex.e: The company was also named Parle after [the suburb of] Vile Parle itself.

ولے پارلے.e: Borrowed from Marathi विले पार्ले (vile pārle).

ومانتل.e: From Sanskrit विमान (vimāna, airplane) + तल (tala, base).

ونا.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit विना (vínā). Doublet of بِن (bin) and بِنَا (binā), a tadbhava.

senses.g: Winder (a city in Lasbela district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

ex.u: ہَم سَب حَضْرَت آدَم کے وَنْشَج ہَیں.
ex.r: ham sab hazrat ādam ke vanśaj ha͠i.
ex.e: We all are the descendants of Hazrat Adam.

وواد.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit विवाद (vivāda). Doublet of بِباد (bibād)

وواہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit विवाह (vivāha). Doublet of بِیاہ (biyāh).

senses.g: he, she, it, they (distal third person subjective personal pronoun)

وہ.e: Uncertain. Most likely from Old Hindi उहु (uhu), उहुं (uhuṃ), from Apabhramsa ओहु (ohu), from Prakrit 𑀅𑀳𑁄 (aho), from Sanskrit असौ (asau, “that”). The inflected forms are formed by analogy with its proximal counterpart یِہ (ye). For Old Hindi वो (vo), Chatterji suggested a derivation from Apabhramsa ओ (o), from Sanskrit *अव (*ava), attested in अवोष् (avoṣ), अवोर् (avor, “of you both”, gen. du.), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *avá. He suggested the term not being preserved in literature but continuing to be spoken. Compare Avestan 𐬀𐬬𐬀 (ava). Note that the second etymology explains plural وے (ve) as well, while the first does not. See there for more.

Uncertain. Most likely from Old Hindi उहु (uhu), उहुं (uhuṃ), from Apabhramsa ओहु (ohu), from Prakrit 𑀅𑀳𑁄 (aho), from Sanskrit असौ (asau, “that”). The inflected forms are formed by analogy with its proximal counterpart یِہ (ye). For Old Hindi वो (vo), Chatterji suggested a derivation from Apabhramsa ओ (o), from Sanskrit *अव (*ava), attested in अवोष् (avoṣ), अवोर् (avor, “of you both”, gen. du.), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *avá. He suggested the term not being preserved in literature but continuing to be spoken. Compare Avestan 𐬀𐬬𐬀 (ava). Note that the second etymology explains plural وے (ve) as well, while the first does not. See there for more.

senses.g: Vehari (a city in Vehari district, Punjab, Pakistan)
there (only in the sense of an abstract noun of place. Urdu does not have a word equivalent to the English usage for "there is...".)

وہم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian وَهْم (wahm), borrowed from Arabic وَهْم (wahm), from وَهَمَ (wahama). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi وہم (vhm /⁠vaham⁠/).

senses.g: he, she, it, they, that, those

ex.u: یہ حَرْکَت وَہی کَر سَکْتا ہَے۔
ex.r: ye harkat vahī kar saktā hai.
ex.e: Only he/it can carry out this act.

ex.u: وہ وَہی آدمی ہَے جِسے مَیں نے دُکان پَر دیکھا تھا۔
ex.r: vo vahī ādmī hai jise ma͠i ne dukān par dekhā thā.
ex.e: He is that very/the same man whom I saw at the shop.

ویتنام.e: Borrowed from English Vietnam, from Vietnamese Việt Nam.

ex.u: وَیدِک زَمانے میں گوشْت کھانے کے رِواج چَلْتے تھیں
ex.r: vaidik zamāne mẽ gośt khāne ke rivāj calte thẽ
ex.e: Ritual of meat eating was prevailed in vedic era.

ویر.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit वीर (vīra). Doublet of بیر (ber).
Borrowed from Sanskrit वीर (vīra). Doublet of بیر (ber).

ویریہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit वीर्य (vīrya). Doublet of بِیرَج (bīraj)

وے.e: Inherited from Old Hindi वे (ve), वै (vai), from Apabhramsa ओइ (oi), perhaps ultimately from Sanskrit *अव (*ava), attested in अवोष् (avoṣ), अवोर् (avor, “of you both”, gen. du.), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *avá. Compare Avestan 𐬀𐬬𐬀 (ava).

senses.g: The thirty-fourth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by و and followed by ھ.
ہ (he) with hamza, see ء. Usually used for izāfa.
force (power; strength, of a nation for instance)

ہاتھ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ہاتھ (hath) c. 1503 / ہات (hāt) c. 1503 / ہَتھ (hath) / ہَت (hat), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀢𑁆𑀣 (hattha), from Sanskrit हस्त (hasta), from Proto-Indo-Iranian, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰés-to- (“hand”), from *ǵʰes-. Doublet of دَسْت (dast). Cognate with Punjabi ہَتھّ / ਹੱਥ (hattha). Wider cognates include Pashto لاس (lâs), Persian دست (dast).

senses.g: to have sleight of hand; for one to have craftsmanship / skill in their occupation or line of work.
to have a defence for an act.

ex.u: نہ ان کے دل میں رحم ہے اور نہ ان کے ہاتھ میں صفائی ہے۔
ex.r: Ḥasb-i maʻmūl
ex.e: There is neither mercy in their heart nor do they have any justification for their actions.

ہاتھ میں صفائی ہونا.e: From ہَاتھ (hāth, “hand”) + میں (mẽ, “in”) + صَفَائی (safāī, “cleanliness”) + ہونا (honā, “to have”), literally “to have cleanliness in hands”.

ہاتھی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀢𑁆𑀣𑀺 (hatthi), from Sanskrit हस्तिन् (hastin).

ہاٹ.e: First attested in c. 1780 as Middle Hindi ہاٹ (haṭ) and Old Hindi हाट (hāṭa) in c. 1420, inherited from Prakrit 𑀳𑀝𑁆𑀝 m (haṭṭa), 𑀳𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀻 f (haṭṭī), from Sanskrit हट्ट m (haṭṭa, “market; fair”), हट्टी f (haṭṭī, “small market or fair”). Cognate with Bengali হাট (haṭ), Gujarati હાટ (hāṭ), Punjabi ہٹ / ਹੱਟ (haṭṭ), and Sindhi هَٽُ (haṭu).

ہار.e: Inherited from Prakrit हारि (hāri), from Sanskrit हारि (hāri, “defeat; losing a game”).

senses.g: Hartlepool (a town and seaport in County Durham, in northeastern England)

ex.u: یَہاں تَشْرِیف لانے کے لِیے آپ کو ہارْدِک مُبارَک باد!
ex.r: yahā̃ taśrīf lāne ke liye āp ko hārdik mubārak bād!
ex.e: A heartfelt welcome to you here for your honorable presence!

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

ہارون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَارُون (hārūn), borrowed from Arabic هَارُون (hārūn), borrowed from Biblical Hebrew אַהֲרֹן (ʔahăron).

senses.g: Haroonabad (a city in Bahawalnagar district, Punjab, Pakistan)
haar, the third month of the Punjabi calendar.
Hollywood (an area of Los Angeles, California, United States, known as the center of the American motion picture industry)
A handi, a type of cooking pot typically made of earthenware or metal.
A dish such as a dal or curry.

ہانڈی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi هانڐي (hanḍy) (c. 1621), from Sanskrit हण्ड (haṇḍa). Cognate with Malvi हाँड़ी (hā̃ṛī), Bengali হাঁড়ী (hā̃ṛī), Marathi हंडा (haṇḍā).

senses.g: driving off or away (by shouting or bawling)

ہانک.e: Inherited from Sanskrit हक्क (hakka, “calling to an elephant”). Cognate with Gujarati હાંક (hā̃k), Marathi हाक (hāk).

senses.g: Hong Kong (a city, island and special administrative region in southeastern China)

ex.u: ہَاں یِہ صَحِیح ہَے
ex.r: hā̃ ye sahīh hai
ex.e: Yes, this is right

ہاں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀸𑀫𑁆𑀫 (hāmma), from Sanskrit आम् (ām).

ex.u: ہَاں کَہْہ دو۔
ex.r: hā̃ kah do.
ex.e: Say yes.

ہاں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀸𑀫𑁆𑀫 (hāmma), from Sanskrit आम् (ām).

ہائیڈروجن.e: Borrowed from English hydrogen, borrowed from French hydrogène, from hydro- + -gène.

ex.u: ہائے ہائے!
ex.r: hāe hāe!
ex.e: alas!

ہائے.e: Inherited from Sanskrit हायि (hāyi). Cognate with Marathi 𑘮𑘰𑘧 (hāy) / हाय (hāy), Sindhi 𑋞𑋠𑊲 (hāi) / Sindhi हाय (hāy), Kumaoni हाइ, Punjabi ਹਾਏ (hāē) / ہائے (hā'e).

senses.g: an exclamation of distress; a sigh

ہائے.e: Inherited from Sanskrit हायि (hāyi). Cognate with Marathi 𑘮𑘰𑘧 (hāy) / हाय (hāy), Sindhi 𑋞𑋠𑊲 (hāi) / Sindhi हाय (hāy), Kumaoni हाइ, Punjabi ਹਾਏ (hāē) / ہائے (hā'e).

ہتیارا.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit हत्याकारक (hatyākāraka). By surface analysis, ہَتْیَا (hatyā) + آرَا (ārā).
Borrowed from Sanskrit हत्याकारक (hatyākāraka). By surface analysis, ہَتْیَا (hatyā) + آرَا (ārā).

ہتھیار.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀢𑁆𑀣𑀺𑀬𑀸𑀭 (hatthiyāra), from Sanskrit *हस्तकार (*hastakāra), from हस्त (hásta, “hand”) + कार (kāra, “effort, exertion”). Compare Punjabi ਹਥਿਆਰ (hathiāra) / ہَتھیار (hatheyār), Sindhi هَٿِيارُ (hathīāru) / हथिआरु, Gujarati હથિયાર (hathiyār), Marathi हत्यार (hatyār), Bengali হাতিয়ার (hatiẏar), Dhivehi ހަތިޔާރު (hatiyāru).

ہتھیلی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ہتیلی (hatelī) (c. 1528), from Sanskrit हस्ततल (hastatala) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-). Compare ہاتھ (hāth, “hand”).

senses.g: direct plural of ہَتھیلی (hathelī, “palm of the hand”)
vocative plural of ہَتھیلی (hathelī, “palm of the hand”)
oblique plural of ہَتھیلی (hathelī, “palm of the hand”)

ex.u: وہ ہٹا کٹا بچہ ہے۔
ex.r: voh haṭṭā-kaṭṭā baccā hai.
ex.e: He is a strong boy.

ہٹنا.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *हट्टइ (haṭṭaï) of uncertain origin.

senses.g: separation, parting, absence (from a beloved, friend or family)
isolation (ie. to invoke a spiritual and religious relationship with God by shielding oneself from materialistic values)

ہجر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَجْر (hajr), borrowed from Arabic هَجْر (hajr), from هَجَرَ (hajara). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi ہجر (hjr /⁠hajr⁠/). Doublet of ہِجْرَت (hijrat).

ہجری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هجری (hijrī), from Arabic هِجْرِيّ (hijriyy, “of or relating to the Hijra/Hegira”).
Borrowed from Classical Persian هجری (hijrī), from Arabic هِجْرِيّ (hijriyy, “of or relating to the Hijra/Hegira”).

ex.u: ماضی میں ہجووں کے نشر پر زیادہ سخت پابندیاں ہوتی تھیں۔
ex.r: māzī mẽ hajvõ ke naśr par zyāda saxt pābandiyā̃ hotī thī̃.
ex.e: In the past, there used to be greater restrictions on the publication of lampoons.

senses.g: lampoon, satire — a lampoonish genre of Urdu poetry

ہجو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هجو (hajw), from Arabic هَجْو (hajw).

ہجے.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هِجَاء (hijā'), from Arabic هِجَاء (hijāʔ)

ہچکنا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀳𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘𑁆 (*hicc). Compare Punjabi ਹਿਚਕਣਾ (hickaṇā) / ہِچکݨا (hickṇā), Gujarati હીંચકવું (hī̃cakvũ), હીંચકો (hī̃cko), Marathi हिसकणे (hisakṇe).

ہچکی.e: Derived from the verb ہِچَکْنَا (hicaknā), which is from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀳𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘𑁆 (*hicc).

ہخامنشی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَخَامَنِشِی (haxāmanišī). By surface analysis, ہَخامَنِش (haxāmaniś, “Achaemenes”) + ـی (-ī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian هَخَامَنِشِی (haxāmanišī). By surface analysis, ہَخامَنِش (haxāmaniś, “Achaemenes”) + ـی (-ī).

ہدایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هِدَایَت (hidāyat), borrowed from Arabic هِدَايَة (hidāya), from هَدَى (hadā). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi ہدایت (hdayt /⁠hidāyat⁠/).

senses.g: alternative form of ہدایت کار (hdāet kār)

ہدیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَدِیَه (hadiya), from Arabic هَدِيَّة (hadiyya). Compare Bengali হাদিয়া (hadiẏa).

ہڈی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀳𑀟𑁆𑀟 (haḍḍa) (see there for more) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), or directly borrowed from Sanskrit हड्ड (haḍḍa). By surface analysis, ہَڈّا (haḍḍā) + ـی (-ī). Cognate with Punjabi ہَڈّی (haḍḍī).

ہر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هر (har). Doublet of سب (sab).

senses.g: everywhere, at every place; on every occasion

ex.u: ڈُھون٘ڈ لیتی ہَیں مُجھے ہَرْجَا میری رُسْوائِیاں۔
ex.r: ḍhū̃ḍ letī ha͠i mujhe harjā merī rusvāiyā̃.
ex.e: My infamies manage to find me everywhere.

ہرجا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَرْجَا (harjā, literally “every place”).

ہرجائی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَرْجَائِی (harjā'ī) / هَرْجَایِی (harjāyī). By surface analysis, ہَرْجَا (harjā, “everywhere”) + ـِی (-ī).

senses.g: tramp, a fickle lover, flirt, casanova, philanderer

ex.u: جو مسلمان تھا اللہ کا سودائی تھا۔ کبھی محبوب تمھارا یہی ہرجائی تھا۔
ex.r: jo musalmān thā allāh kā saudāī thā. kabhī mahbūb tumhārā yahī harjāī thā.
ex.e: The one who was a Muslim was lovesick of God. Once was this very tramp Thy beloved.

ہرجائی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَرْجَائِی (harjā'ī) / هَرْجَایِی (harjāyī). By surface analysis, ہَرْجَا (harjā, “everywhere”) + ـِی (-ī).

ہردی.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit हृदय (hṛdaya), from Proto-Indo-European *ḱḗr.
